What you see on my screen is a blank value proposition design canvas. I want to walk you through the structure of it so you understand the framework. There's nothing here right now. This is just to see the overall framework that we'll be using. What I want to draw your attention to first is that there are two parts. On the right hand side, that circle represents the audience. That's not an enormous audience. It's whatever audience you have defined that you want to create a value proposition for. It could be a large audience. It could be a slice of an audience. It's really anything where the combination of characteristics that we'll identify is different so that you want to create an offer specifically for that audience. And then on the left hand side, what we have is the actual product or the service. You'll see that I've named these creative professionals on the right and then the traffic engine core training on the left. This whole canvas would be an audience of creative professionals and then the core product, not the upsell for agencies, but the core product for the traffic engine. We work through this in a specific order. We start on the right and we will identify the customer jobs to be done. We will do that. And you don't need to use this particular framework. This is a free tool that I use. You can do this with a whiteboard. And I think often it's better to do it with a whiteboard or a piece of paper and post-it notes or other things because it forces you to think a little bit differently. We'll do both. So on the right, we have customer jobs. We would put in post-its or I'll show you an example of one in a moment what it would look like to indicate one very specific job. It could be functional, it could be social, it could be emotional, but it needs to be very clear. And then after that, within the context of this audience's jobs to be done, we look at the pains, the problems associated with satisfying those jobs. And we'll provide some prompts for each of these so don't worry about writing any of this down. After we've identified the pains, we move on to gains. What is it that from these jobs, this particular audience hopes to gain from it? And those gains can be social and emotional and transactional, functional as well. That map that we create gives us a very deep understanding of what really motivates the audience. Not because we want to give them all of that. That's really where the magic of the value proposition design Canvas comes from is that we're only going to identify for that particular audience what we think is the most important one to three jobs, the most important one to three pains, and the most important one to three gains. And then we're going to craft and offer a value proposition that maps our product or service and our pain relievers and our gain creators precisely to the pains and gains and jobs that this particular audience, we have identified matters to them. That's really the magic of this entire, when this comes together, that's what makes it so powerful is we're looking at an audience, we're looking at our product or service, and we're saying what matters to this audience are these few things and they really matter. And then we can look at our product or service and assuming that it does those few things, we can then create an offer that specifically maps our pain relievers to the pains that the audience experiences and does not want to experience the really big ones, the ones that matter. And then we can map our gain creators to the gains that they want to obtain. And we can also map the functional elements of our product to the customer jobs to be done. That's what makes this so powerful. And we will, you'll notice on the left that the core training isn't going to change. It is what it is all the pain relievers and gain creators are there can they're consistent for the product. But when we bring in different audiences on the right, maybe a new audience would be an agency owner or someone who wants to become a service provider. Well then their jobs, their pains and their gains change dramatically, which is an opportunity to match those for that specific audience with a value proposition that resonates most with them. So using this tool, we can just add something and we can type something in and then we will put lots of these, we can move them around and we can organize those on the framework itself. And then if we want to later, we can color code what we think are the top one to three in each to make it easier, or we can just screenshot it. I like to take a screenshot and then go work with it by hand and to start working through what I think are the most important factors. So I'm sharing this now just so you understand as I as I write about it, that there is a literal framework here. The shapes and the order in which we go through them, all of that matters. And as you go through this, you're going to see the power that this framework offers.
